retrieved_docs.title: Diffuse Axonal Injury -- Treatment / Management -- Investigational Treatments Targeting Secondary Axotomy
retrieved_docs.content: Secondary axotomy represents an inflammatory-apoptotic process triggered by cytoskeletal disruption and calcium overload. Preclinical studies have identified several agents that may blunt this cascade. Calcium-channel blockade with nimodipine and calcineurin inhibition by ciclosporin A both attenuate β-APP accumulation, preserve mitochondrial integrity, and reduce axonal swelling. Calpain inhibitors provide variable protection, while FK506 (tacrolimus) significantly limits cytoskeletal damage and, when combined with controlled hypothermia, may extend the therapeutic window. More recent agents, eg, SN-6 (NCX1 blocker) and dynasore (Drp1/dynamin inhibitor), mitigate stretch-induced varicosities, mitochondrial degradation, and oxidative injury, and microtubule stabilizers (eg, paclitaxel or Epothilone D) have been shown to forestall secondary depolymerization and swelling. However, blood–brain barrier penetration remains a challenge. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Central Pontine Myelinolysis -- Treatment / Management -- Experimental Strategies
retrieved_docs.content: The use of glucocorticoids, such as dexamethasone, has also been studied as glucocorticoids are known to impact the permeability of the blood-brain barrier. Studies done in rat models showed minimal neurologic impairment in rats that were treated with dexamethasone 2mg/kg at 0 and 6 hours after hypertonic 3% saline was administered. The rats that were treated with dexamethasone also exhibited rare lesions on brain imaging in comparison to the untreated group, which exhibited numerous brain lesions. There have been few cases where dexamethasone was used in humans. However, this is not well studied, and further investigation is warranted. [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Anatomy, Head and Neck: Blood Brain Barrier -- Other Issues -- Current Strategies for Delivery of Drugs Across the BBB
retrieved_docs.content: Ongoing research focuses on developing new strategies and techniques for drugs to effectively cross the BBB and deliver therapeutic products in the CNS. These techniques can be classified into invasive and non-invasive or miscellaneous techniques.

retrieved_docs.content: Interestingly, a case series demonstrated that the use of extracranial picotesla range pulsed electromagnetic fields (EMFs) effectively treated patients with Lhermitte's sign. One theory is that the reduction of axonal excitability occurs through the modulation of ionic membrane permeability. The second theory involves modulating pain control systems through neurotransmitter activity and pineal melatonin functions, as discussed earlier. [22] [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Ventral Cord Syndrome -- Pertinent Studies and Ongoing Trials
retrieved_docs.content: Emerging research is focusing on measures which could help prevent ischemic spinal cord injury during vascular surgery. Hypothermia preserves tissue integrity during periods of interrupted blood flow; animal studies suggest that hypothermia suppresses excitatory synaptic transmission and reduces injury to neurons. Hyperbaric oxygen increases tissue oxygenation and metabolism in penumbral tissue. Ischemic preconditioning is a way to prepare tissue for the ischemic state, thus rendering it more resistant to a hypoxic insult. Numerous preclinical investigations have looked at the molecular pathways and potential benefits of both pre and post-ischemic preconditioning with encouraging results. Other studies are examining how to counteract apoptosis which leads to neuronal cell death.

retrieved_docs.title: Allodynia -- Evaluation -- Neuronal Function Tests
retrieved_docs.content: Somatosensory evoked potentials measure electrical activity in the brain after a somatosensory stimulus to beta fibers. This test assesses the health of a neuron as a whole and is often useful when possible CNS conduction issues are suspected, such as in patients with multiple sclerosis or spinal cord injury, and in the neurosurgical operating room to prevent injury. retrieved_docs.title: Targeted Temperature Management  -- Anatomy and Physiology -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Reperfusion causes a massive increase in the production of free radicals such as hydrogen peroxide, superoxide, nitric oxide, and hydroxyl radicals. The high levels overwhelm the defensive antioxidant mechanisms throughout the body and cause the peroxidation of lipids, proteins, and nucleic acids, which contribute to neuronal damage. [8] One study using an in vitro model of cerebral ischemia found that the neuroprotective effects of hypothermia were associated with a significant reduction in nitric oxide and superoxide formation when temperatures were reduced to 31 to 33 C. [9] The inflammatory response that follows reperfusion has both beneficial and detrimental effects, with some mediators being transiently neuroprotective. However, this exaggerated response may last up to 5 days, and persistently high levels of cytokines are destructive over this protracted time course. Hypothermia suppresses the inflammatory cascade and, in turn, prevents the exacerbation of cerebral injury by inflammation.

retrieved_docs.content: Transient cerebral ischemia is characterized by a transitory clinical course, indicating that the neurological deficits associated with this brief interruption of cerebral blood flow are temporary and typically resolve quickly without permanent damage.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 2678
retrieved_docs.content: Injury of central nervous system due to birth trauma is a condition in which the pathological finding is located within the central nervous system. The central nervous system is anatomically linked to the dura mater, as the dura mater constitutes a physical component of the protective meningeal layers that surround the brain and spinal cord. Consequently, injuries to the central nervous system, such as those caused by birth trauma, involve structures that are encased by the dura mater, underscoring the importance of the dura mater in shielding the central nervous system and influencing clinical assessment and management of such injuries.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 1079
retrieved_docs.content: Encephalitis is described as being located in the brain (mozek). The brain itself serves as the anatomical site for several pathological findings, including metabolic brain disorder (اعتلال دماغي أيضي) and brain compression (انْضِغاطُ الدِّماغ). In addition, the brain is identified as the location of toxic brain disorder (اعتلال دماغي سمي). Beyond structural pathology, the brain is also linked to the psychological process of feeling guilt (الشعور بالذنب), indicating its role in emotional and cognitive functions. Therapeutically, the brain is the direct procedural site for electroconvulsive therapy (العلاج بالتخليج الكهربي), a treatment that targets neural activity. Together, these relationships highlight the brain’s central involvement in infectious, metabolic, compressive, toxic, and emotional conditions, as well as its importance as a target for specific neuropsychiatric interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2404
retrieved_docs.content: Encephalitis is a condition that occurs in the brain (mozek). The brain (mozek) also serves as the anatomical site for several pathological findings, including metabolic brain disorder (اعتلال دماغي أيضي), brain compression (انْضِغاطُ الدِّماغ), and toxic brain disorder (اعتلال دماغي سمي). These relationships highlight that the brain is both the location of encephalitis and the locus where these specific brain injuries or dysfunctions are identified, underscoring the importance of evaluating the brain for multiple concurrent or related pathologies in clinical assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2226
retrieved_docs.content: Traumatisch hersenoedeem is a specific subtype of أوديما دماغية, meaning that traumatic brain edema is classified under the broader condition of cerebral edema. As a form of أوديما دماغية, Traumatisch hersenoedeem involves swelling of brain tissue that results from head injury, leading to increased intracranial pressure and potential neurological impairment. Recognizing Traumatisch hersenoedeem as a subset of أوديما دماغية helps clinicians understand its pathophysiology within the context of general cerebral edema, guiding appropriate diagnostic evaluation and targeted therapeutic interventions to reduce brain swelling and protect neurological function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2567
retrieved_docs.content: Laser interstitial thermal therapy (LITT) performed under imaging guidance is classified as an “other or gastrointestinal therapeutic procedure.” This minimally invasive technique uses laser energy to thermally ablate targeted tissue, with real‑time imaging (such as MRI or CT) providing precise navigation and monitoring. By falling within the broader category of other or gastrointestinal therapeutic procedures, LITT is recognized as a specialized treatment option for lesions in gastrointestinal and adjacent structures, offering a focused alternative to more invasive surgical interventions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1005
retrieved_docs.content: Encephalitis is a condition that occurs in the brain (mozek). The brain serves as the anatomical site for several pathological findings, including metabolic brain disease (اعتلال دماغي أيضي) and brain compression (انْضِغاطُ الدِّماغ). It is also the location where cerebral edema (أوديما دماغية), general brain disease (اعتلال دماغي), and toxic brain disease (اعتلال دماغي سمي) can develop. Functionally, the brain is the locus of the cognitive process of thinking (myšlení) and is linked to the emotional experience of guilt (الشعور بالذنب). Clinically, the brain is the direct target for electroconvulsive therapy (العلاج بالتخليج الكهربي). This integrated view underscores the brain’s central role in disease manifestation, neurocognitive functions, emotional processing, and therapeutic interventions.
